skill.name: error-handling
skill.description: Guides structured error handling for FastAPI backends and React or Next.js frontends, including custom exceptions, global handlers, logging, and user-facing API errors.
skill.summary: Implement consistent backend and frontend error handling patterns.
skill.author: Awais68

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high-risk patterns, but AI review found the weak cryptography, shell execution, network, filesystem, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from documentation examples or regex validation. The only residual risk is a local verification script that reads files from a supplied skill path and performs no network or code execution.

low_findings.title: Local Validation Script Reads a Supplied Path
low_findings.description: scripts/verify.py accepts an optional command-line path, reads SKILL.md from that path, and checks for scripts/verify.py. This is a legitimate local validation helper with no network access or code execution, but it is executable content bundled with the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script behavior is clear from the argument parsing, file reads, and existence checks. Confidence is below very high because the risk depends on how marketplace users choose to execute the helper.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Regex and Text Matches
low_findings.description: The reported lines do not use cryptographic algorithms. scripts/verify.py uses regular expressions to parse frontmatter, and SKILL.md contains documentation text and example identifiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review confirmed no MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic primitive at the cited locations. The matches are caused by regex syntax, Markdown frontmatter, and ordinary example text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution alerts are Markdown inline-code spans and fenced code blocks in SKILL.md. They document Python and TypeScript examples and are not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited SKILL.md locations are visible Markdown formatting and examples, not Ruby source files. No instruction asks the model to execute these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch call is part of a frontend API client example that builds a same-origin URL. The second network alert points to a rate-limit message in backend example text, not an HTTP client call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch example uses window.location.origin and does not name an external endpoint. No evidence of credential collection, beaconing, or data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Filesystem Alerts Are Relative Imports
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts refer to TypeScript import paths in a documentation snippet. They do not read files, write files, normalize user paths, or access parent directories at runtime in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are import declarations inside a Markdown code block. There is no filesystem API or user-controlled path operation at those locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Ordinary Identifiers
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts point to validation output, example IDs, SQL placeholders, and callback parameter names. No command collects hostnames, users, environment details, process lists, or network configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no OS or network reconnaissance behavior at the cited locations. The matches are ordinary application examples and validation messages.

content.value_statement: Teams often leak technical details or handle API failures inconsistently. This skill provides backend and frontend patterns for predictable errors, logging, and user-facing messages.
actual_capabilities: Defines FastAPI exception hierarchies with status codes and user messages.
Shows global backend exception handlers for application and unexpected errors.
Provides structured logging patterns with correlation IDs and stack traces.
Maps API errors to user-friendly frontend messages in React or Next.js.
Demonstrates API client and React Query error handling patterns.
Includes a quality checklist for logging, status codes, and response formats.
limitations: It provides patterns and examples, not a packaged runtime library.
It focuses on FastAPI, React, Next.js, and React Query conventions.
It does not replace domain validation or security review of error payloads.
Example code may need adaptation for each project structure and framework version.

use_cases.title: Standardize API Errors
use_cases.description: Create consistent exception classes, status codes, and response messages across FastAPI services.

use_cases.title: Improve Frontend Failure States
use_cases.description: Map API failures to clear titles, messages, toasts, and retry behavior in React applications.

use_cases.title: Review Full-Stack Error Flows
use_cases.description: Check that logging, user messages, and internal details are separated before release.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Error Classes
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new backend service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design basic FastAPI error classes for not found, validation, unauthorized, forbidden, conflict, and rate limit cases.

prompt_templates.title: Add Frontend Error Messages
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to map API error responses into user-friendly React messages, titles, and toast behavior for common status codes.

prompt_templates.title: Connect Backend and Frontend Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to align FastAPI exception responses with a React API client, including response format, fallback messages, and logging boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Existing Error Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review an existing full-stack error handling design for swallowed errors, sensitive logs, inconsistent status codes, and unclear user messages.

output_examples.input: A FastAPI service returns different error shapes from each endpoint.
output: A shared exception hierarchy with clear status codes.
A global handler that returns one response shape.
A logging plan that keeps internal details out of user messages.

output_examples.input: A React app shows the same generic message for every failed request.
output: A frontend error mapper for authentication, permissions, missing resources, conflicts, and rate limits.
A toast strategy that uses friendly messages without exposing internal details.

output_examples.input: A team needs a release checklist for error handling quality.
output: Checks for logged or surfaced exceptions, correct status codes, correlation IDs, and sensitive data controls.
Recommendations for consistent API error formats across backend and frontend code.

best_practices: Keep technical details in logs and return simple user-facing messages to clients.
Use narrow exception handling so expected errors and unexpected failures stay separate.
Add correlation IDs so backend logs and frontend reports can be traced together.
anti_patterns: Do not swallow broad exceptions without logging or surfacing the failure.
Do not expose stack traces, tokens, passwords, or personal data in API responses.
Do not use one generic status code for validation, authentication, authorization, and server failures.

faq.question: Does this skill install an error handling package?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that you adapt to your project.

faq.question: Which backend framework does it target?
faq.answer: The backend examples target FastAPI and Python exception handling patterns.

faq.question: Which frontend tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers React or Next.js patterns, including API clients, error mappers, toasts, and React Query hooks.

faq.question: Can it help prevent sensitive error leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes separate internal details, user messages, and logging boundaries.

faq.question: Does it replace business validation logic?
faq.answer: No. It supports error handling around validation results, but domain rules still belong in application logic.

faq.question: Is the bundled verification script required?
faq.answer: No. The script only checks the skill file structure and frontmatter when run locally.
